content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 15 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 107r–108v, LC PDF pages 212–213 | **Source Images:** DEU_015_page_001.jpg, DEU_015_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0153 — DEU_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** At the end of seven years you shall make a release [shemittah].

**2** And this is the manner of the release [shemittah]: every creditor shall release what he has lent to his neighbor; he shall not press his neighbor and his brother, for the LORD's release has been proclaimed.

**3** The foreigner [nokri] you may press, but that which is yours with your brother your hand shall release.

**4** But there will be no poor among you—for the LORD will surely bless you in the land which the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance to possess—

**5** only if you listen carefully to the voice of the LORD your God, to keep and to do all this commandment that I am commanding you today.

**6** For the LORD your God will bless you, as he spoke to you, and you will lend to many nations but you will not borrow, and you will rule over many nations but they will not rule over you.

**7** If there is among you a poor man, one of your brothers within one of your gates, in your land which the LORD your God is giving you, you shall not harden your heart and you shall not shut your hand from your poor brother.

**8** Rather you shall surely open your hand to him and you shall surely lend him sufficient for his need in whatever he lacks.

**9** Guard yourself lest there be a word in your heart, a worthless [belial] thing, saying, "The seventh year, the year of release, draws near," and your eye is evil against your poor brother and you give him nothing, and he cries out to the LORD against you and there is sin in you.

**10** You shall surely give to him, and your heart shall not be grieved when you give to him, for on account of this thing the LORD your God will bless you in all your work and in all your endeavor.

**11** For the poor will not cease from the midst of the land; therefore I am commanding you, saying, "You shall surely open your hand to your brother, to your afflicted and to your poor in your land."

**12** If your brother, a Hebrew man or a Hebrew woman, is sold to you and serves you six years, then in the seventh year you shall send him out from with you, free [chofshi].

**13** And when you send him out free from with you, you shall not send him out empty-handed.

**14** You shall surely adorn him from your flock and from your threshing floor and from your winepress; as the LORD your God has blessed you, you shall give to him.

**15** And you shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt and the LORD your God redeemed you; therefore I am commanding you this thing today.

**16** And it shall be, if he says to you, "I will not go out from with you," because he loves you and your household, for it is good for him with you—

**17** then you shall take the awl and put it through his ear and through the door, and he shall be for you a servant forever. And also to your female servant you shall do thus.

**18** Let it not be hard in your eyes when you send him out free from with you, for at double the wage of a hired worker he has served you six years; and the LORD your God will bless you in all that you do.

**19** Every firstborn male [bekhor] that is born in your herd and in your flock you shall consecrate [taqdiish] to the LORD your God; you shall not work with the firstborn of your ox and you shall not shear the firstborn of your flock.

**20** Before the LORD your God you shall eat it year by year in the place which the LORD will choose, you and your household.

**21** And if there is in it a blemish—lameness or blindness, any severe blemish—you shall not sacrifice it to the LORD your God.

**22** In your gates you shall eat it—the unclean and the clean alike—as [you would eat] the gazelle and the deer.

**23** Only its blood you shall not eat; on the ground you shall pour it out like water.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1 — shemittah:** "Release." The noun shemittah (שְׁמִטָּה) derives from the verb shamat, "to let drop/release." EMET preserves it with the gloss "release." This is the debt-release law, distinct from the Sabbatical year (shnat ha-sheva) of Lev 25. LXX Vaticanus (p. 153) ἄφεσιν — "release," agreeing.

**v.2 — shemot kol-ba'al masheh yado:** "Every creditor shall release what he has lent" — literally "every master of a loan of his hand shall release." The idiom ba'al masheh, "master/owner of a loan," is standard Hebrew for creditor. LC reads clearly; LXX ἀφήσεις πᾶν χρέος ἴδιον.

**v.4 — efes ki lo yihyeh bekha evyon:** "But there will be no poor among you" — the tension between this verse and v.11 ("the poor will not cease") is famous. EMET renders both literally. V.4 states the promised ideal under obedience; v.11 acknowledges the present reality.

**v.7 — lo ta'ametz et-levavekha:** "You shall not harden your heart" — literally "you shall not make your heart strong/resistant." The verb ametz normally means "to be strong/courageous"; here it has the connotation of hardening. Compare Pharaoh's hardened heart.

**v.9 — davar belial:** "A worthless thing" — belial (בְּלִיַּעַל) in compound with davar means "a word of worthlessness/wickedness." EMET retains "belial" as a technical term with gloss. The "evil eye" (ve-ra'ah 'einekha) means to look upon with miserliness.

**v.11 — anaw and evyon:** "Afflicted and poor" — two distinct terms: anaw (עָנִי) denotes one who is bowed down/afflicted; evyon (אֶבְיוֹן) denotes the one who is destitute/needy. Both appear in the LC.

**v.12 — eved ivri:** "A Hebrew servant/slave." The technical term for the indentured Israelite. EMET uses "servant" in the translation body. Ivri likely refers to ethnic/legal status. LXX (p. 153) δοῦλος Ἑβραῖος.

**v.14 — ha'aneq ta'aniq lo:** "You shall surely adorn him" — the cognate infinitive absolute ha'aneq intensifies the verb. The root 'anaq means to put a neck-chain on, hence to adorn/furnish lavishly. LXX ἐφόδιον ἐφοδιάσεις αὐτόν — "provision you shall provision him."

**v.17 — martzea' ve-natata be'ozno u-va-delet:** "Awl... through his ear and through the door" — the door-piercing ceremony makes the servant's bond both public and permanent. The awl through the ear to the door is a literal act of inscription upon the body. LC reads clearly. LXX τρυπητήρι.

**v.19 — taqdiish:** "You shall consecrate" — the Hiphil of qadash, to declare holy/set apart. The firstborn male (bekhor) belongs to the LORD by right of redemption — the logic of Exod 13.

**v.21 — ki yihyeh vo mum:** "If there is in it a blemish" — the unblemished standard for sacrificial animals. Mum (מוּם) = a physical defect. LC lists two examples: pisei'ach (lame) or iver (blind).

**LXX comparison (Vaticanus p. 153):** Vaticanus page 153 contains both the tail of ch. 14 and the whole of ch. 15. The LXX of ch. 15 is closely aligned with MT throughout. Notable: v.14, LXX reads ἐφόδιον ἐφοδιάσεις ("provision you shall provision") for the ha'aneq ta'aniq of MT — the Greek captures the intensifying infinitive absolute with the cognate noun/verb pair. Minor omission in LXX v.3: the phrase "that which is yours with your brother" is slightly compressed.

— *EMET*
